preamble: The voiceless alveolo-palatal affricate ([t͡ɕ]), a sound articulated with the blade of the tongue against the region between the alveolar ridge and the front of the hard palate, can undergo a phonetic transformation to the voiceless postalveolar affricate ([t͡ʃ]). This shift involves a slight retraction of the tongue's point of articulation, resulting in the more common 'ch' sound found in many languages.
phoneticEffects: This phonetic shift is characterized by a backing or retraction of the place of articulation from the alveolo-palatal region to the postalveolar region

examples.note: In some varieties of Croatian, the alveolo-palatal affricate /t͡ɕ/ has merged with the postalveolar affricate /t͡ʃ/, eliminating the alveolo-palatal place of articulation.
In certain Silesian dialects, such as those spoken in Gmina Istebna, the alveolo-palatal affricate /t͡ɕ/ has merged with the postalveolar affricate /t͡ʃ/.

sources: Ćavar, M. (2021). The Phonetics and Phonology of Sibilants: A Synchronic and Diachronic OT Typology of Sibilant Inventories. Rutgers Optimality Archive.
